07-stikkord-samling/dat320/arithmetic-lo.title: Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/arithmetic-lo.description: The **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** is a fundamental component of the CPU responsible for performing **arithmetic** and **logical operations**.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/atomic-operat.title: Atomic Operations
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/atomic-operat.description:
Atomic operations are the building blocks of concurrency control, enabling locks and lock-free algorithms by ensuring indivisible updates to shared memory.One-liner: Hardware-supported instructions that perform read-modify-write in one indivisible step, essential for building correct synchronization primitives. Atomic operations guarantee that a sequence of steps (read, compute, write) happens as a single, uninterruptible unit. No other thread or CPU can observe or interleave during the operation. Used to implement locks and lock-free data structures. Prevent race conditions in critical sections. Enable mutual exclusion without disabling interrupts or relying on OS scheduling. Foundation for spin locks and lock-free algorithms. Test-and-Set (TAS) Atomically sets a memory location to 1 and returns its old value. Used in basic spin locks. Example: old = test_and_set(&flag).
Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Atomically compares a memory location to an expected value and, if equal, updates it. Returns success/failure. Example: CAS(&flag, 0, 1).
Fetch-and-Add (FAA) Atomically increments a memory location and returns the old value. Used in ticket locks for fairness. Example: ticket = fetch_and_add(&next_ticket, 1). Other primitives Load-Linked / Store-Conditional (LL/SC): Used in some architectures for building atomic sequences. Atomic Exchange: Swap values in one atomic step. Correctness: Guarantees atomicity at hardware level. Performance: Fast compared to OS-based blocking, but can cause cache contention under heavy load. Fairness: Depends on how the primitive is used (e.g., TAS is unfair, FAA can enforce FIFO). Implementing spin locks (TAS, CAS). Building fair locks (FAA ticket locks). Creating lock-free data structures (CAS for stacks, queues). Atomic counters and reference counting.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/cache-coheren.title: Cache Coherence & Affinity
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/cache-coheren.description: Cache Coherence: A hardware-level mechanism ensuring that all CPUs see a consistent view of memory when they have private caches. If CPU1 modifies a cached value, other CPUs must not read stale data. Implemented via protocols like MESI (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid). Cache Affinity (Processor Affinity): A scheduling policy that tries to keep a process on the same CPU where it previously ran. Reason: The CPU’s cache likely contains data the process needs. Goal: Reduce cache misses and improve performance. Multi-CPU Systems: Each CPU has its own cache. When processes migrate between CPUs: Cache contents are lost → more memory accesses. Increased latency and reduced throughput. Performance Impact: Maintaining affinity improves cache hit rate. Reduces memory bus traffic and coherence overhead. Load Balancing vs Affinity: Keeping affinity may conflict with balancing CPU load. Example: A CPU is overloaded, but moving tasks breaks affinity. Cache Coherence Overhead: Even with affinity, shared data between threads on different CPUs requires coherence traffic. Hardware protocols handle this but add complexity. Affinity Scheduling: Soft Affinity: Prefer same CPU but allow migration. Hard Affinity: Pin process to a CPU (e.g., taskset in Linux). Work Stealing: Used in multi-queue schedulers (e.g., Go runtime). Tries to balance load while minimizing unnecessary migrations. Cache affinity is a policy for performance optimization. Cache coherence is a hardware mechanism for correctness. Both are critical in multi-core systems for efficiency and consistency.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/code-segment..title: Code segment
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/code-segment..description: The code segment contains the program instructions that the CPU executes. It stores the compiled machine code of the process and is typically read-only to prevent accidental modification during execution. Since the code remains mostly unchanged while the program runs, it can often be shared between multiple instances of the same program to save memory.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/compare-and-s.title: Compare-and-Swap
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/compare-and-s.description:
CAS spin locks are correct and more versatile than TAS, but still unfair and wasteful under contention. CAS shines in lock-free algorithms, not as a basic spin lock.One-liner:
A lock built on the atomic compare_and_swap instruction; threads spin until they successfully change the lock state from “free” to “held.”
Primitive:
compare_and_swap(addr, expected, new) atomically: Compares the value at addr with expected.
If equal, sets it to new.
Returns the old value. Lock idea:
Use a shared flag (0 = free, 1 = held). Acquire: Loop calling compare_and_swap(&flag, 0, 1) until it succeeds.
Release: flag = 0. volatile int flag = 0; void lock() { while (!compare_and_swap(&flag, 0, 1)) { // spin }
} void unlock() { flag = 0;
} Yes: Mutual exclusion guaranteed because CAS is atomic and only succeeds if the flag was 0. Fair? No (starvation possible).
Like Test-and-Set, CAS does not enforce ordering; a new thread can repeatedly win the race. Single CPU: Poor under preemption (same issue as TAS).
Multi-CPU: Better than TAS for some patterns because CAS can be used for lock-free algorithms, but still spins under contention. Enables lock-free synchronization for certain data structures (e.g., atomic counters, stacks).
Unlike TAS, CAS can check and update in one atomic step without blindly setting the value. Correct mutual exclusion.
Hardware-supported on most modern CPUs.
Foundation for lock-free algorithms. Busy-waiting wastes CPU cycles.
Unfair; starvation possible.
Cache contention under heavy load. Backoff strategies to reduce contention.
Hybrid locks (spin briefly, then block).
Use CAS for lock-free data structures instead of simple spin locks.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/completely-fa.title: Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/completely-fa.description: CFS is the default scheduling algorithm in the Linux kernel. It aims to fairly distribute CPU time among processes by tracking how much CPU time each process has used and selecting the one with the least "virtual runtime" (vruntime).(Note: replaced with something else from year 2025 in Linux Kernal 2.6.6)

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/concurrency.h.title: Concurrency
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/concurrency.h.description:
Definition: Concurrency is about dealing with many things at once, whereas parallelism is about doing many things at once. Concurrency focuses on correctness and coordination, while parallelism focuses on speed and resource utilization.Key Concepts: Threads vs Processes: Threads share the same address space within a process, making communication easier but introducing complexity (e.g., race conditions). Critical Section: A piece of code that accesses shared resources and must not be executed by more than one thread simultaneously. Mutual Exclusion: Ensures only one thread executes in the critical section at a time. Atomicity: Operations that cannot be interrupted, essential for implementing locks. Disable Interrupt Idea: Disable interrupts when entering the critical section. Correctness: Works on single CPU, fails on multi-CPU. Fairness: No (depends on well-behaved threads). Performance: Poor.
Load and Store (Basic Spin-Waiting) Correctness: No (race conditions possible even with atomic load/store). Fairness: No. Performance: Poor.
Test-and-Set (TAS) Spin Lock Correctness: Yes. Fairness: No (starvation possible). Performance: Poor on single CPU, better on multi-CPU.
Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Spin Lock Correctness: Yes. Fairness: No (starvation possible). Performance: Similar to TAS. Note: Enables lock-free synchronization for some operations.
Fetch-and-Add Lock Correctness: Yes. Fairness: Yes (FIFO ordering possible). Performance: Still involves spinning.
Yield-Based Lock Idea: Threads yield CPU instead of spinning. Fairness: Better than spin locks but not guaranteed. Issues: Unnecessary context switches.
Queue-Based Lock (Park/Unpark) Idea: Threads block and are queued; OS wakes them in order. Fairness: Yes (FIFO). Performance: Good (avoids spinning).
Semaphore Idea: A counter-based synchronization primitive that can act as a lock or signal mechanism. Correctness: Yes (mutual exclusion or controlled concurrency). Fairness: Depends on implementation (often FIFO in OS). Performance: Efficient (threads block instead of spinning).
Condition Variable Idea: Allows threads to wait for a condition and be signaled when it changes. Correctness: Yes (when paired with a mutex and used correctly). Fairness: Depends on implementation (usually FIFO wakeup). Performance: Efficient (threads sleep instead of busy-waiting). Correctness: Provides mutual exclusion. Fairness: Prevents starvation; treats waiting threads equally. Performance: Minimizes overhead under different loads and CPU configurations.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/condition-var.title: Condition Variable
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/condition-var.description:
Condition variables let threads sleep until a condition is met, avoiding busy-waiting and enabling efficient coordination. Always pair with a mutex and re-check conditions after waking.One-liner:
A synchronization primitive that lets threads wait for a condition to become true without busy-waiting, using an explicit queue and signaling mechanism.
A condition variable is paired with a mutex.
Threads can: Wait: Block until a condition is met.
Signal: Wake one or more waiting threads when the condition changes. Used to coordinate threads when simple mutual exclusion isn’t enough (e.g., producer-consumer).
var cond = sync.NewCond(&mu) func producer() { mu.Lock() while bufferFull { cond.Wait() // releases mu and blocks } // produce item cond.Signal() // wake one waiting thread mu.Unlock()
} func consumer() { mu.Lock() while bufferEmpty { cond.Wait() // releases mu and blocks } // consume item cond.Signal() mu.Unlock()
} Wait(): Assumes the mutex is locked.
Atomically: Releases the mutex.
Puts the thread to sleep. When woken, re-acquires the mutex before returning. Signal(): Wakes one waiting thread (or Broadcast() wakes all).
Must hold the mutex when signaling. Yes: Prevents race conditions by combining mutex and condition checks.
Important: Always check the condition in a loop after waking (spurious wakeups possible). Fair? Depends on implementation.
Most OSes wake threads in FIFO order, but fairness is not guaranteed by the concept. Efficient: Threads block instead of spinning.
Overhead: Mutex acquisition and context switches. Avoids busy-waiting.
Enables complex coordination (e.g., producer-consumer, ordering constraints).
Works with existing mutexes. Requires careful usage: Must hold the lock when calling Wait() or Signal().
Must re-check condition after waking. Misuse can lead to deadlocks or missed signals. Producer-Consumer: Wait when buffer is full/empty.
Thread ordering: Wait until another thread completes a task.
Resource availability: Wait until a resource becomes free.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/cpu-cycle.htm.title: CPU Cycle
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/cpu-cycle.htm.description: CPU cycles between Fetch, Decode and Execute instructions/Code received

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/cpu-register-.title: CPU Register Values
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/cpu-register-.description: CPU register values are the contents stored in a process's CPU registers at a given moment. Registers are small, high-speed storage locations inside the CPU that hold important information such as instruction addresses, data, and processor status. In a Process Control Block (PCB), the operating system saves the register values during a context switch so that the process can later resume execution exactly where it left off. This saved state typically includes the program counter, stack pointer, general-purpose registers, and status registers.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/cpu-virtualiz.title: CPU Virtualization
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/cpu-virtualiz.description: Virtualizing the CPU is a process that will allow a single CPU to run multiple programs at the same time.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/dead-lock.htm.title: Dead-Lock
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/dead-lock.htm.description:
Deadlock is a cycle of waiting that halts progress. The Dining Philosophers problem illustrates how resource ordering and careful design prevent deadlock.One-liner:
A situation in concurrent systems where a set of threads are blocked forever, each waiting for a resource held by another.Deadlock occurs when:
Two or more threads hold resources and wait for each other to release resources in a cycle.
None can proceed because each is waiting for a resource that will never be released.
Deadlock can occur if all four hold:
Mutual Exclusion: Resources cannot be shared; only one thread can use a resource at a time.
Hold and Wait: A thread holds one resource while waiting for another.
No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken away.
Circular Wait: A cycle of threads exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next.
Scenario:
Five philosophers sit around a table.
Each needs two forks (left and right) to eat.
Each philosopher picks up the left fork, then waits for the right fork.
Why Deadlock Happens:
All philosophers pick up their left fork simultaneously.
Each waits for the right fork (held by neighbor).
Circular wait forms → deadlock. Break Circular Wait: Impose an ordering on resource acquisition (e.g., always pick up the lower-numbered fork first).
Limit concurrency: Allow only 4 philosophers to sit at the table at once.
Use a waiter (central authority): Philosophers request both forks at once.
Timeouts: If a philosopher cannot acquire both forks, release and retry later. Deadlock: No progress forever.
Starvation: A thread waits indefinitely because others keep getting priority.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/disable-inter.title: Disable Interrupt
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/disable-inter.description:
Disabling interrupts as a lock is only a uniprocessor-era trick: correct on single CPU, incorrect on SMP; unfair; and risky for system latency. Prefer OS-supported mutexes or atomic-instruction-based locks.One‑liner:
Turn off timer/interrupt delivery while a thread is in the critical section so it can’t be preempted.A historical mutual‑exclusion technique where a thread (or the kernel) disables interrupts before entering a critical section and re‑enables them after exiting. With interrupts off, the running context cannot be preempted on that CPU, preventing involuntary context switches during the critical section.
Simplicity: No data structures, no queues, no atomic instructions required.
Determinism (on uniprocessors): If you can’t be preempted, no other thread runs, hence no races on shared memory. Single CPU: Correct for mutual exclusion (no other thread can run while interrupts are disabled).
Multiple CPUs: Not correct by itself. Each CPU has its own interrupt-enable bit. Disabling interrupts on one CPU doesn’t stop other CPUs from running and touching the same shared memory. Fair? No.
There is no ordering or queueing of waiters. Progress depends entirely on when the current holder re-enables interrupts and on scheduling policy afterwards. A misbehaving thread can monopolize the CPU. Poor / risky, especially: Long critical sections: You block timer interrupts ⇒ no preemption ⇒ potential scheduling jitter and latency spikes.
Multiprocessors: Doesn’t solve the problem; you’d still need other mechanisms, and busy CPUs continue running.
System impact: Disabling interrupts delays device handling and timekeeping; overuse can cause missed deadlines and degraded I/O responsiveness. Acceptable (niche): Very short, bounded critical sections in a uniprocessor kernel or early boot code.
Critical hardware manipulation that must be atomic with respect to interrupt handlers on the same CPU. Avoid for: User-space code.
Multiprocessor systems.
Any non-trivial critical section or when latency/SLA matters. // Conceptual only — user space should NOT do this
disable_interrupts(); // e.g., cli() on some architectures
// --- critical section ---
... manipulate shared state ...
// --- end critical section ---
enable_interrupts(); // e.g., sti() Caveat: In real kernels you must also save/restore the prior interrupt state (nesting-safe) and ensure you don’t sleep or block while interrupts are disabled. Extremely simple conceptually.
Works without special hardware atomic instructions (on single CPU).
Zero memory contention (because no one else runs). Not SMP-safe: Fails to provide mutual exclusion across CPUs.
Unbounded latency: Interrupts deferred; can harm real-time constraints and device servicing.
No fairness / risk of starvation.
Error-prone: Forgetting to re-enable interrupts = system stall; enabling when already enabled can break nesting; calling code that sleeps while interrupts are off can deadlock the system.
Not portable to user space. SMP or user space: Use proper mutexes (queue-based/OS-supported).
Short critical section on SMP: Use spinlocks built on atomic instructions which also disable preemption and, in kernels, may mask interrupts locally if required (but still coordinate across CPUs).
Contention expected / long sections: Use blocking mutexes (park/unpark) or read–write locks; consider lock-free algorithms where appropriate.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/fetch-and-add.title: Fetch-and-Add

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/file-system.h.title: File System
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/file-system.h.description: Contains machine code instructions for bootstrapping the OS. Loads the OS kernel during system startup. Located at the very beginning of the disk. Stores file system metadata: File system type Number of inodes and data blocks Block size Helps the OS interpret and organize files on the storage device. Driver: Handles communication between OS and physical device (read/write commands). Bitmap tracking free/used inodes. Each bit corresponds to an index in the Inode Table: 0 = free, 1 = used. Limit: Number of bits = max number of files/directories. Example: 64-bit I-bmap → max 64 files/directories. Bitmap tracking free/used data blocks. Each bit corresponds to a data block index: 0 = free, 1 = used. Limit: Number of bits × block size = total storage capacity. Example: 128 bits × 4 KB = 512 KB. A structured collection (conceptually a “table”) of inode records. Each inode describes: File type (regular file, directory, etc.) Permissions, owner, timestamps Size Pointers to data blocks (direct, indirect, multi-level indexing for large files). Represents a file or directory. Identified by inode number, not by name. Names are resolved via directory lookups. Stores actual file content or directory entries. Directory data blocks contain name → inode number mappings, not file content. Block size determined by file system (e.g., 4 KB). I-bmap → Inode Table → Inode → Data Blocks D-bmap → Data Blocks Directories use data blocks for mappings, not file content. Max files = number of inodes (I-bmap size). Max storage = number of data blocks × block size. You can run out of inodes before space, or vice versa. Bitmaps (I-bmap, D-bmap) or free lists. Open: Resolves path by reading directory inodes and data blocks recursively. Read: Uses inode pointers to fetch data blocks. Write: Updates bitmaps, inode metadata, and block pointers. Abstraction layer for system calls. Allows multiple file systems (ext4, FAT, etc.) under one interface. Speeds up reads/writes by caching frequently accessed blocks. Interrupts: Reduce CPU overhead vs polling. DMA (Direct Memory Access): Transfers large chunks without CPU copying each word. App calls write(fd, buf, size). Kernel maps fd → file object → inode. VFS dispatches to specific file system driver. File system driver updates inode and bitmaps, prepares block I/O. Generic block layer sends request to block device driver. Disk controller uses DMA to transfer data. Interrupt signals completion. Imagine you’re saving a new file called report.txt to your disk. Here’s what happens behind the scenes: Checking the Maps The file system first consults the I-bmap (Inode Bitmap) to find a free inode. This bitmap is like a seating chart for inodes—each bit represents whether an inode is free or occupied. It finds an empty spot and marks it as used. Consider it as the seat is now reservered for an Inode Creating the Inode Next, the system creates an inode in the Inode Table at the corresponding (reserved) position found in I-bmap. This inode is the file’s identity card—it stores metadata like file type, permissions, owner, timestamps, and most importantly, pointers to data blocks where the actual content will live. Finding Space for Data The file system then checks the D-bmap (Data Bitmap) to locate free data blocks. These blocks are the actual storage units for your file’s content. It picks a few empty blocks and marks them as used. Writing the Content Your text—“Hello, world!”—is written into those data blocks. The inode’s pointers are updated to reference these blocks, so the system knows where your file’s data resides. Updating Metadata The inode updates its size and timestamps. The superblock might also update global counters (like free block count), ensuring the file system stays consistent. Mission Complete Now, when you open report.txt, the OS looks up the inode, follows its pointers to the data blocks, and retrieves your content.

headers.heading: The Journey of Writing a File (AI-Copilot + update by me)

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/first-in,-fir.title: First In, First Out (FIFO)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/first-in,-fir.description: **First In, First Out (FIFO)** is a simple, non-preemptive scheduling algorithm where processes are executed in the order they arrive. It is one of the earliest and most intuitive scheduling strategies.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/fragmentation.title: fragmentation
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/fragmentation.description:
Fragmentation refers to wasted memory space that cannot be effectively used for allocation.
This waste can occur inside allocated blocks (internal) or between allocated blocks (external).Occurs when allocated memory blocks are larger than required, leaving unused space inside the block. This space cannot be used by other processes.Occurs when free memory is split into small, scattered chunks. Even if total free memory is sufficient, it cannot be allocated to a process that needs a large contiguous block.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/free-space-ma.title: Free Space Management
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/free-space-ma.description: The OS, through some decision logic, allocated memory space when a process is created or when a process ask for more memory space through a system call often called malloc(size). The logic is usually to give the process som segment of the physical memory to handle as it wishes. The logic defines how well the space is managed and if it experiences a high or low fragmentation. This is a system call by a process to the OS asking to be allocated more memory. Memory can only be released when the process terminates or if the process calls free() on some memory.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/heap.html.title: Heap
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/heap.html.description: The heap is a region of memory used for dynamic memory allocation during program execution. Memory in the heap is allocated and released as needed, allowing data structures such as linked lists, trees, and objects to grow or shrink at runtime. The heap typically expands toward higher memory addresses and is managed by the operating system and memory allocation routines.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/input-output-.title: Input-Output (IO)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/input-output-.description:
I/O devices are devices connected to the computer that we wish to input data into or receive its output.
Example is a keyboard where a user inputs manual keystrokes which is outputted to the machine.
Another example is a storage device where we input requests for data and the storage device outputs the requested data.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/io-communicat.title: IO communication
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/io-communicat.description:
I/O-devices are slow relative to the computational speed of a CPU. Therefore it would be a problem if the CPU spends time waiting for the I/O-device to become available for use if its busy or waiting on its expected output (This is called polling, it continually asks "are you ready?"). Therefore the solution is to allow the I/O-device to cause an interrupt and say that it is ready to the OS. This way we can block a process while it is waiting for I/O to signal it is ready and the OS will switch the process back to Ready. Sometimes however we do want the CPU to spin-wait a little while. This is when the I/O-device is deemed "fast" so that by the time an I/O request is finished within the time-frame of a context-switch so that a context-switch has to occur again to handle the interrupt caused by the I/O-request finishes. Softlock: This occurs where there is a large series of outputs occuring in rapid succession and therefore causing so many interruptions that you are stuck context-switching and unable to handle the incomming data. So polling when the CPU wants to will avoid the softlock. Therefore we want to have both spin-waiting and blocked waiting. This is done by allowing a short spin-wait too see if it immediately finishes before interrupting the process to block it.I/O-devices might themselves wait a little before sending an interrupt. This is called coalescing and it means to collect some amount of data over a certain time before causing an interrupt and can therefore send a chunk of data at once. Problem becomes finding the magic "waiting time" to avoid feeling that the I/O is "lagging"This is a device within a system with the task of transferring data between memory and an I/O device. Without this we would need to dedicate CPU-time to transfer data. CPU-time is valuable and copying data is trivial, so therefore DMA was created. Problem CPU is spending time copying data.Solution
The CPU tells the DMA what set of data to copy and the DMA handles the copying and data transfer. Processes cannot be given total control over I/O-devices. If they did they would interfere with each other and in worst case become a portal for malicious activity to take over the machine. Therefore the OS must handle the communication or limit a processes ability to interact with it. The OS allows a process some control over an I/O-device by placing some of the controls in memory where the process can access it. The OS handles all communication between process and deviceA problem with I/O devices is how to communicate with them correctly. There are hundreds of thousands of different devices, each expecting to be communicated to in a specific way. Therefore we have device drivers that handles as an interpreter between the device and the OS. The OS wants to communicate in the same way to all devices so it is up to the driver to interprete the OS commands into something the device expects and vice versa.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/io-status.htm.title: IO status
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/io-status.htm.description: I/O status information in a Process Control Block (PCB) contains details about the input/output resources assigned to a process. This may include a list of open files, allocated devices, pending I/O requests, and device status information. The operating system uses this data to manage the process's access to hardware resources and to resume I/O operations correctly when the process is scheduled to run again.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/load-and-stor.title: Load and Store
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/load-and-stor.description:
Load-and-store locks are unsafe and should never be used for mutual exclusion. They illustrate why atomic instructions (Test-and-Set, CAS) are essential for correct locking.One-liner:
Attempt mutual exclusion by reading a flag (load) and writing it (store) without advanced atomic instructions.A naive lock implementation that uses a shared flag variable:
Acquire: Check if flag == 0; if so, set flag = 1.
Release: Set flag = 0.
Threads spin (busy-wait) until the flag becomes available.
Simplicity: Uses only basic memory operations (load and store).
No hardware support needed: Works on any architecture with basic memory instructions. Fails even with atomic load/store assumptions.
Example: Two threads can both read flag == 0 before either writes flag = 1. Both enter the critical section → race condition. Fair? No.
There is no ordering; any thread that wins the race enters first. Others spin indefinitely, risking starvation. Poor: Spins continuously, wasting CPU cycles.
On single CPU, if the holder is preempted, others spin for an entire time slice.
On multi-CPU, contention causes cache thrashing. // INCORRECT lock using load/store
while (flag == 1) { // spin
}
flag = 1; // enter critical section
// ...
flag = 0; // release Atomicity violation: Load and store are separate steps; between them, another thread can intervene.
Interrupts/context switches: Make timing unpredictable. Extremely simple conceptually.
No special hardware instructions required. Not correct: Cannot guarantee mutual exclusion.
No fairness: Starvation possible.
Performance: Wastes CPU cycles; scales poorly.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/lottery-sched.title: Lottery Scheduling
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/lottery-sched.description: **Lottery Scheduling** is a probabilistic, proportional-share scheduling algorithm. Each process is assigned a number of "tickets" representing its share of the CPU. The scheduler randomly selects a ticket to decide which process runs next.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/memory.html.title: Memory
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/memory.html.description: Memory is an array of bytes where each byte has a specific address where it stores data.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/memory-manage.title: Memory Management Info
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/memory-manage.description: Memory management information in a Process Control Block (PCB) contains details about the memory allocated to a process and how it is organized. This information may include page tables, segment tables, base and limit registers, and pointers to memory regions used by the process. The operating system uses this data to manage virtual memory, enforce memory protection, and translate logical addresses into physical addresses. This ensures that processes can access their own memory safely without interfering with other processes.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/mmu.html.title: MMU
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/mmu.html.description:
The Memory Management Unit (MMU) is a hardware component that manages memory access between the CPU and main memory. Its primary function is to translate virtual addresses generated by a process into physical addresses in RAM. This allows each process to operate within its own virtual address space without needing to know where its data is physically stored.The MMU works closely with the page table and the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB). When the CPU requests access to a memory location, the MMU first checks the TLB for a cached address translation. If the translation is not found, the MMU retrieves the mapping from the page table. The MMU also enforces memory protection, preventing processes from accessing memory regions that belong to other processes or to the operating system.Without the MMU, modern features such as virtual memory, paging, memory protection, and process isolation would not be possible. It is a critical component in modern operating systems, ensuring both efficient and secure memory management.Key concepts:
Address Translation: Converts virtual addresses to physical addresses.
Memory Protection: Prevents unauthorized memory access.
Virtual Memory Support: Enables processes to use virtual address spaces.
Works with TLB and Page Tables: Provides fast and efficient memory access.
Location: Hardware component between the CPU and main memory.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/multi-level-f.title: Multi-Level Feedback Queue (MLFQ)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/multi-level-f.description: **MLFQ** is a dynamic, preemptive scheduling algorithm that uses multiple queues with different priority levels. It adapts to process behavior over time, aiming to balance responsiveness and fairness without prior knowledge of job characteristics.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/multi-queue-m.title: Multi-Queue Multi-CPU Scheduling (MQMS)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/multi-queue-m.description: MQMS is a scheduling strategy for multi-core systems where each CPU has its own independent scheduling queue. This approach improves cache affinity and scalability by reducing contention and allowing localized scheduling decisions.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/paging.html.title: Paging
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/paging.html.description:
Paging is a memory management scheme that eliminates the need for contiguous allocation of physical memory by dividing both virtual memory and physical memory into fixed-size blocks:
Pages: Fixed-size blocks in virtual memory.
Frames: Fixed-size blocks in physical memory.
A page frame can hold exactly one page. Avoid external fragmentation caused by variable-sized segments.
Simplify free space management by using uniform block sizes.
Enable virtual memory: processes can use more memory than physically available. Virtual Address (VA): Split into: VPN (Virtual Page Number): Index into the page table.
Offset: Location within the page. Physical Address (PA): Derived from: PFN (Page Frame Number): From page table entry.
Offset: Same as VA offset. Maps VPN → PFN for each process.
Stored in memory; MMU uses Page Table Base Register to locate it.
Each Page Table Entry (PTE) contains: PFN (physical frame number)
Present bit: Indicates if page is in memory or on disk.
Protection bits: Read/Write/Execute permissions.
Dirty bit: Modified since load.
Accessed bit: For replacement policies. Extract VPN and offset from VA. If VA is 32 bits and frame size is 4KB Then 4Kb needs bits for the offset So VPN is bits and offset is 12bits. Use VPN to index into page table → get PFN.
Combine PFN + offset → Physical Address. Every memory access requires: Page table lookup (extra memory access).
Then actual data access. Solution: Use TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer): A small, fast cache of recent VPN → PFN mappings.
Reduces overhead by avoiding frequent page table lookups. Multi-level Page Tables: Reduce memory overhead for sparse address spaces.
Inverted Page Tables: Use a global table indexed by PFN.
Large Pages: Reduce table size but cause internal fragmentation. Eliminates external fragmentation.
Supports virtual memory and process isolation.
Enables flexible allocation and swapping. Page table can be large (e.g., 4 MB per process for 32-bit VA with 4 KB pages).
Requires hardware support (MMU, TLB).
Page faults and disk I/O can be expensive.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/persistence.h.title: Persistence
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/persistence.h.description:
In computer engineering, persistence refers to the characteristic of data that outlives the process that created it. In other words, persistent data is saved in a non-volatile storage medium (like a hard drive, SSD, or database) so that it remains available even after a program ends or the computer is turned off. Volatile vs. Non-Volatile: Volatile memory (like RAM) loses data when power is lost.
Non-volatile memory (like disk storage) retains data — this is where persistence happens. Persistent Storage Examples: Files saved on disk (e.g., .txt, .json, .csv)
Databases (e.g., MySQL, MongoDB)
Cloud storage (e.g., AWS S3, Google Drive) Why It Matters: Without persistence, all data would be lost every time a program stops.
It enables long-term data storage, backups, and recovery. A note-taking app saves your notes to a file or database. Even if you close the app or restart your computer, your notes are still there — thanks to persistence.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/process.html.title: Process
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/process.html.description: A Process is a running program

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/process-contr.title: Process Control Block (PCB)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/process-contr.description:
Concept:
The Process Control Block (PCB) is a data structure used by the operating system to manage and track the state of a process. It acts as the process’s identity card, containing all the information the OS needs to control and schedule the process.Key Contents of a PCB:
Process ID (PID): Unique identifier for the process.
Program Counter (PC): Address of the next instruction to execute.
CPU Register Values: Snapshot of the CPU state (e.g., ACC, general-purpose registers).
Process State: Current status (e.g., running, ready, waiting).
Memory Management Info: Pointers to memory segments (code, data, stack).
Scheduling Info: Priority, CPU time used, etc. Vruntime I/O Status: List of I/O devices allocated to the process.
Role in Context Switching: During a context switch, the OS saves the current process’s CPU state into its PCB and loads the state of the next process from its PCB. This enables multitasking and process isolation.Why It Matters:
The PCB is essential for process management, multitasking, and resource allocation in modern operating systems. Without it, the OS couldn’t keep track of multiple processes or resume them correctly.Related Concepts:
Context Switching
CPU Registers
Scheduler
Operating System Kernel
Connections:
↔️ CPU Cycle
↔️ ACC (Accumulator)
↔️ Context Switching

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/process-id-(p.title: Process ID (PID)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/process-id-(p.description: A PID (Process Identifier) is a unique number assigned by the operating system to each running process. The PID allows the operating system to identify, manage, and track processes individually. It is used for tasks such as scheduling, resource management, process communication, and terminating processes. Since every active process has its own PID, the operating system can distinguish between multiple processes running at the same time.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/process-state.title: Process State
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/process-state.description: The process state indicates the current status of a process during its execution. Common states include New (being created), Ready (waiting for CPU time), Running (currently executing on the CPU), Waiting/Blocked (waiting for an event such as I/O completion), and Terminated (finished execution). The operating system stores the process state in the Process Control Block (PCB) so it can manage scheduling decisions and track the progress of each process.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/queue-based-l.title: Queue-Based Lock
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/queue-based-l.description:
Queue-based locks are correct, fair, and efficient, making them the preferred choice for blocking mutexes in modern OSes and user-space libraries.One-liner:
A lock that avoids spinning by blocking threads in a queue and waking them in order when the lock becomes available.
Instead of busy-waiting or yielding, threads that cannot acquire the lock park (block) and are placed in a queue.
When the lock is released, the next thread in the queue is unparked (moved to ready state).
Requires OS support for blocking and waking threads. Acquire: Try to get the lock.
If busy: Acquire a small guard lock to manipulate the queue.
Add self to queue.
Call park() (block). Release: Acquire guard.
Remove next thread from queue.
Call unpark(next) to wake it.
Leave the lock flag set so the woken thread can enter immediately. Yes: Mutual exclusion guaranteed because only one thread holds the lock at a time. Fair? Yes (FIFO).
Threads are woken in the order they queued, preventing starvation. Excellent under contention: No spinning → no CPU waste.
Scales well for long waits. Overhead: Context switches and OS calls, but far better than burning CPU cycles. Fairness: FIFO ordering.
Efficiency: Threads sleep instead of spinning.
Scalable: Handles high contention gracefully. Requires OS primitives (park, unpark, or condition variables).
More complex than simple spin locks.
Slightly higher latency for very short critical sections compared to spinning.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/round-robin-(.title: Round Robin (RR)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/round-robin-(.description: **Round Robin (RR)** is a preemptive scheduling algorithm designed for time-sharing systems. Each process is assigned a fixed time slice (quantum) and the CPU cycles through the ready queue in a circular fashion.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/scheduling.ht.title: Scheduling
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/scheduling.ht.description: **Scheduling** is the method by which an operating system decides which process gets to use the CPU and for how long. It balances performance, fairness, responsiveness, and resource utilization.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/scheduling-in.title: Scheduling info
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/scheduling-in.description: Scheduling information in a Process Control Block (PCB) contains data used by the operating system's scheduler to determine when and how a process should use the CPU. This information may include the process priority, scheduling queue pointers, CPU usage statistics, and scheduling parameters. The scheduler uses this data to select the next process to run, ensuring efficient CPU utilization and fair allocation of processing time among all processes.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/semaphore.htm.title: Semaphore
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/semaphore.htm.description:
Semaphores are powerful and versatile, replacing locks and condition variables in one abstraction. They block threads instead of spinning, making them efficient under contention.One-liner:
A synchronization primitive that generalizes locks and condition variables into a single mechanism for controlling access and signaling.
A semaphore is an integer value with two atomic operations: P (wait/down): Decrement the value; if it becomes negative, block the thread.
V (signal/up): Increment the value; if there are waiting threads, wake one. Used to: Limit concurrent access to resources.
Signal between threads (ordering, producer-consumer). Binary Semaphore: Value initialized to 1.
Acts like a mutex (only one thread in critical section). Counting Semaphore: Value initialized to N.
Allows up to N threads to access a resource concurrently. // Initialize
Semaphore s = 1; // binary semaphore // Acquire
P(s); // wait/down // Critical section
... // Release
V(s); // signal/up Yes: Provides mutual exclusion or controlled concurrency depending on initial value. Fair? Depends on implementation.
Most OS implementations wake threads in FIFO order → fair.
But fairness is not guaranteed by the concept itself. Efficient: Threads block when they cannot proceed (no spinning).
Overhead: Requires OS support for blocking and waking. Combines capabilities of locks and condition variables.
Can implement: Mutual exclusion.
Producer-consumer synchronization.
Ordering constraints. Simple abstraction for many concurrency patterns. More complex than basic locks.
Misuse can lead to deadlocks or priority inversion.
Harder to reason about than explicit locks + condition variables. Binary semaphore: Protect critical sections.
Counting semaphore: Limit access to a pool of resources.
Signaling: Thread A waits for an event; thread B signals when event occurs.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/shortest-job-.title: Shortest Job First (SJF)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/shortest-job-.description: **Shortest Job First (SJF)** is a non-preemptive scheduling algorithm that selects the process with the shortest total execution time. It aims to minimize average turnaround time by prioritizing shorter jobs.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/shortest-time.title: Shortest Time to Completion First (STCF)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/shortest-time.description: **Shortest Time to Completion First (STCF)** is a preemptive scheduling algorithm that always selects the process with the least remaining execution time. It is the preemptive counterpart to Shortest Job First (SJF).
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/shortest-time.sourcePath: 07 - Stikkord samling/DAT320/Shortest Time to Completion First (STCF).md

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/single-queue-.title: Single Queue Multi-CPU Scheduling (SQMS)
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/single-queue-.description: SQMS is a scheduling strategy used in multi-CPU systems where all CPUs share a single global scheduling queue. Each CPU pulls tasks from the same queue, aiming for simplicity and centralized control.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/stack.html.title: Stack
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/stack.html.description: The stack is a memory region used to store function calls, local variables, and return addresses. It follows a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) structure, meaning the most recently added data is accessed first. The stack grows and shrinks automatically as functions are called and completed, making it an efficient way to manage temporary data during program execution. It typically grows toward lower memory addresses.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/stride-schedu.title: Stride Scheduling
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/stride-schedu.description: **Stride Scheduling** is a deterministic proportional-share scheduling algorithm. It ensures fair CPU allocation by assigning each process a stride value based on its share of CPU time (tickets). The process with the lowest accumulated "pass" value is selected to run next.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/test-and-set..title: Test-and-Set
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/test-and-set..description:
TAS spin locks are correct but unfair and can be wasteful. Prefer ticket locks for fairness or blocking mutexes (queue‑based) for efficiency under contention.One‑liner:
A lock built on the atomic test_and_set instruction; threads busy‑wait until the lock flag becomes free.
Primitive: test_and_set(addr) atomically reads the old value at addr and sets it to 1 in one indivisible operation.
Lock idea: Use a shared flag (0 = free, 1 = held). Acquire: Loop calling test_and_set(&flag) until it returns 0.
Release: flag = 0. // Shared
volatile int flag = 0; // Acquire
void lock() { while (test_and_set(&flag) == 1) { // spin (busy-wait) }
} // Release
void unlock() { flag = 0; The atomicity of test_and_set guarantees that only one thread observes 0 and sets it to 1 at a time. Yes: Provides mutual exclusion on single and multi‑CPU systems because the check‑and‑set happens atomically. Fair? No (starvation possible).
There’s no ordering among waiters; a newly arriving thread can repeatedly “win” the race when the lock is released, starving others. Single CPU: Poor if the holder is preempted; all other threads spin for an entire time slice with no chance of progress.
Multiple CPUs: Better than on single CPU (a spinning thread may acquire the lock as soon as it’s freed), but still suffers from cache contention and wasted cycles under heavy contention. Simple to implement and reason about.
Requires only one atomic instruction (widely available in hardware).
Correct mutual exclusion without OS support. Busy‑waiting wastes CPU cycles.
Unfair; can cause starvation.
Scales poorly under contention (cache‑line bouncing).
Not suitable for long critical sections or when the holder may block/sleep. Use for: Very short critical sections in low‑contention paths; kernel/embedded contexts where sleeping isn’t allowed and latency is critical.
Avoid for: User‑space locks with potential blocking, longer sections, or high contention—use queue‑based mutexes (park/unpark) or ticket locks (fetch‑and‑add) instead. Test‑and‑test‑and‑set: First spin on flag being 0 using ordinary loads; only call test_and_set when it looks free → reduces atomic contention.
Backoff (exponential): Delay between failed attempts to reduce bus/cache thrashing.
Hybrid locks: Spin briefly, then park (block) to avoid wasting CPU.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/tlb.html.title: TLB
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/tlb.html.description:
The Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) is a small, high-speed cache located inside or very close to the Memory Management Unit (MMU). Its purpose is to speed up the translation of virtual addresses into physical addresses during memory access.When a program accesses memory, the CPU generates a virtual address. Instead of searching the page table in main memory every time, the MMU first checks the TLB. If the required page translation is found (TLB hit), the physical address can be obtained immediately, resulting in much faster memory access. If the translation is not found (TLB miss), the MMU must consult the page table in memory, which takes additional time. The missing translation is then typically added to the TLB for future use.Because memory accesses occur extremely frequently, the TLB significantly improves system performance by reducing the number of page table lookups. Modern processors rely heavily on TLBs to make virtual memory systems practical and efficient.Key concepts:
TLB Hit: Page translation found in the TLB; fast address translation.
TLB Miss: Translation not found; page table lookup required.
Purpose: Reduce address translation overhead and improve memory performance.
Location: Inside or closely integrated with the MMU and CPU.

07-stikkord-samling/dat320/work-stealing.title: Work Stealing
07-stikkord-samling/dat320/work-stealing.description: Work Stealing is a dynamic load-balancing technique used in multi-CPU scheduling systems, particularly in Multi-Queue Multi-CPU Scheduling (MQMS). It allows idle CPUs to "steal" tasks from other CPUs' queues to improve overall system utilization.
